title: Cholinergic Antagonists
summary: مضادات الكولين (Cholinergic Antagonists) هي أدوية تحجب مستقبلات الأسيتيلكولين (Acetylcholine receptors). الأتروبين (Atropine) هو النموذج الأساسي ويحجب المستقبلات المسكارينية بشكل تنافسي عكسي. تشمل استخداماته: تشنجات الجهاز الهضمي، بطء القلب، تسمم المبيدات الفسفورية، وتوسيع حدقة العين. بدائل الأتروبين تشمل الإيبراتروبيوم للرئة، والسوليفيناسين للمثانة، والبنزتروبين لمرض باركنسون.

key_drugs.name: Atropine
key_drugs.note: prototype; reversible competitive blocker; crosses BBB

key_drugs.name: Scopolamine (Hyoscine)
key_drugs.note: stronger CNS effect than atropine; used for motion sickness

key_drugs.name: Ipratropium
key_drugs.note: bronchodilator; tolerance develops due to M2 blockade

key_drugs.name: Tiotropium
key_drugs.note: once daily; no tolerance; COPD maintenance

key_drugs.name: Solifenacin
key_drugs.note: urge incontinence; fewer systemic side effects

key_drugs.name: Glycopyrrolate
key_drugs.note: antisecretory in preanesthesia; no CNS effects (4ry ammonium)

key_drugs.name: Benztropine
key_drugs.note: crosses BBB; used for drug-induced Parkinsonism and tremors

key_drugs.name: Tropicamide
key_drugs.note: shorter acting than atropine (6h); preferred for fundus exam

key_drugs.name: Cyclopentolate
key_drugs.note: 24h action; preferred over atropine in adults

key_drugs.name: Physostigmine
key_drugs.note: treats atropine poisoning; antagonizes central and peripheral effects

mnemonics.title: ABCDE - تأثيرات الأتروبين المركزية
mnemonics.body: A = Antiemetic (M1 trigger zone), B = Basal ganglia antiParkinson (M1), C = Cardiorespiratory stimulation (M2 medulla), D = Delirium/hallucinations (high dose), E = Excitation then CNS depression

mnemonics.title: Mad Bat Bone Hare Beet - علامات تسمم الأتروبين
mnemonics.body: تخيل مجنوناً (Mad as a hatter = هلاوس)، يطير كالخفاش الأعمى (Blind as a bat = عمى + جفاف العين)، عظامه جافة (Dry as a bone = جفاف الفم والجلد)، يركض كالأرنب في الحرارة (Hot as a hare = حمى)، ووجهه أحمر كالشمندر (Red as a beet = احمرار الجلد)

mnemonics.title: IT - بدائل الأتروبين للرئة
mnemonics.body: I = Ipratropium (non-selective, tolerance develops, M2 blockade), T = Tiotropium (selective M3, no tolerance, once daily for COPD maintenance)

mnemonics.title: الأتروبين والعين - صورة بصرية
mnemonics.body: تخيل عيناً مفتوحة على آخرها (Mydriasis = اتساع الحدقة) مع عدسة مجمدة لا تتحرك (Cycloplegia = شلل التكيف) وضغط داخلي مرتفع يضغط من الداخل (IOP increased). كل هذا يجعل العين جافة لا تبكي (Dry eye = تثبيط الدموع).

mnemonics.title: SOB - بدائل الأتروبين للمثانة
mnemonics.body: S = Solifenacin, O = Oxybutynin, B = bladder wall relaxation + sphincter contraction → used in nocturnal enuresis and urge incontinence

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of action of Atropine?
flashcards.back: Reversible competitive blockade of muscarinic receptors (non-selective for all subtypes: M1, M2, M3)

flashcards.front: Why does transient bradycardia occur with a low dose of Atropine?
flashcards.back: Low doses block presynaptic M1 receptors at the cardiac ganglion → increased ACh release → transient cardiac inhibition before M2 blockade becomes dominant

flashcards.front: What is the difference between active and passive Mydriasis?
flashcards.back: Passive: Atropine relaxes the constrictor pupillae muscle → pupil dilates. Active: Sympathomimetics contract the dilator pupillae via α1 → pupil dilates

flashcards.front: What is the treatment for Atropine poisoning?
flashcards.back: Physostigmine (reverses both central and peripheral effects) + Diazepam (to control agitation) + cooling blankets for fever in children (not aspirin)

flashcards.front: Why is Scopolamine preferred over Atropine for motion sickness?
flashcards.back: Scopolamine has a stronger CNS effect than Atropine and is more effective in treating vomiting caused by motion sickness

flashcards.front: Why is Atropine preferred over its substitutes in children for cycloplegia?
flashcards.back: The ciliary muscle is powerful in children and requires a strong blocking agent. Atropine substitutes have a weaker effect on accommodation compared to Atropine

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Ipratropium and Tiotropium?
flashcards.back: Ipratropium: non-selective (M2+M3), blocks M2 so tolerance develops. Tiotropium: selective M3, does not block M2 so no tolerance develops; given once daily for COPD maintenance

flashcards.front: What are the selective M1 Atropine substitutes and why are they used?
flashcards.back: Dicyclomine and Pirenzepine: selectively block M1 → reduce gastric HCl secretion → used in peptic ulcer disease

flashcards.front: Why is Glycopyrrolate preferred over Atropine in anesthesia?
flashcards.back: Glycopyrrolate is a quaternary ammonium compound → does not cross the blood-brain barrier → no CNS side effects → safe antisecretory agent

flashcards.front: What are the uses of Benztropine in neurology?
flashcards.back: 1. Treatment of drug-induced Parkinsonism. 2. Adjunct in Parkinson's disease with tremors. 3. Control of sialorrhea (excessive drooling)

flashcards.front: What are the contraindications of Atropine?
flashcards.back: 1. Glaucoma (especially narrow-angle). 2. Enlarged prostate (benign prostatic hyperplasia). 3. Increased intraocular pressure (IOP)

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Tropicamide and Cyclopentolate in duration of action?
flashcards.back: Tropicamide: mydriasis lasts 6 hours. Cyclopentolate: mydriasis lasts 24 hours. Both are preferred over Atropine in adults for easier reversal

flashcards.front: What is the effect of Atropine on the bronchi?
flashcards.back: Bronchodilation by blocking M3 receptors in bronchial smooth muscle

flashcards.front: What is the main reason for Atropine toxicity in children?
flashcards.back: Children are more sensitive to the fever caused by Atropine (which inhibits sweating) → dangerous hyperthermia

flashcards.front: What is the effect of Atropine on the bladder?
flashcards.back: Relaxes the bladder wall (bladder wall relaxation) and contracts the sphincter → urinary retention — dangerous in patients with benign prostatic hyperplasia

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between Atropine and cooling blankets for children in poisoning?
flashcards.back: Atropine inhibits sweating → the body cannot cool itself → temperature rises → cooling blankets are used (not aspirin, as it raises temperature at low doses)

flashcards.front: What are the uses of Solifenacin compared to Atropine in the urinary tract?
flashcards.back: Solifenacin is more uroselective than Atropine → fewer side effects (no dry mouth, no blurred vision) → used for urinary urgency and nocturnal enuresis

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of action of Atropine?
flashcards.back: Reversible competitive blockade of muscarinic receptors (non-selective for all subtypes).

flashcards.front: What is the natural source of Atropine?
flashcards.back: The plant Atropa belladonna.

flashcards.front: What are the ocular effects of Atropine?
flashcards.back: Mydriasis (pupil dilation), cycloplegia (paralysis of accommodation), and increased intraocular pressure.

flashcards.front: What are the cardiac effects of Atropine?
flashcards.back: Tachycardia (vagal blockade); at low doses may cause transient bradycardia (central effect).

flashcards.front: What are the clinical uses of Atropine?
flashcards.back: Bradycardia, organophosphate poisoning, preanesthetic medication (to reduce secretions), and pupil dilation for eye examination.

flashcards.front: What are the contraindications of Atropine?
flashcards.back: Narrow-angle glaucoma and prostatic hypertrophy (urinary retention).

flashcards.front: What is the use of Ipratropium?
flashcards.back: Antimuscarinic bronchodilator in asthma and COPD (inhaled).

flashcards.front: What is the use of Scopolamine?
flashcards.back: Prevention of motion sickness — antimuscarinic that crosses the BBB.

flashcards.front: What is the use of Homatropine?
flashcards.back: Short-acting mydriatic agent for ophthalmic examination.

mcqs.q: A 3-year-old child ingested eye drops containing Atropine. Which of the following treatments is most appropriate?
mcqs.explanation: Physostigmine is a cholinesterase inhibitor that reverses both the central and peripheral effects of Atropine. Neostigmine does not cross the blood-brain barrier and therefore cannot treat central symptoms. Pralidoxime is used for organophosphate poisoning.

mcqs.q: A patient with COPD was prescribed an inhaled anticholinergic drug. After prolonged use, no tolerance developed. Which drug is most likely?
mcqs.explanation: Tiotropium is selective for M3 and does not block presynaptic M2 receptors, so it does not increase ACh release and tolerance does not develop. Ipratropium is non-selective and blocks M2, leading to tolerance.

mcqs.q: A woman was prescribed an anticholinergic drug for motion sickness. She subsequently experienced vivid dreams and hallucinations. Which drug is most likely?
mcqs.explanation: Scopolamine has a stronger central effect than Atropine and may cause vivid dreams and hallucinations, especially when used as a premedication in female patients.

mcqs.q: A 60-year-old patient suffers from urinary urgency and urge incontinence. Which drug is most appropriate as a selective M3 antagonist?
mcqs.explanation: Solifenacin is a uroselective M3 blocker with fewer systemic side effects (no dry mouth, no blurred vision). It is used for urinary urgency and urge incontinence.

mcqs.q: A child has acute iridocyclitis. Which mydriatic drug is preferred due to its shorter duration of action?
mcqs.explanation: Tropicamide has a duration of action of only 6 hours compared to Atropine which may last up to 7 days, making reversal of the effect much easier.

mcqs.q: What is the physiological basis for passive Mydriasis caused by Atropine?
options: Contraction of the dilator muscle by sympathetic stimulation
Relaxation of the constrictor pupillae by M3 blockade
Stimulation of α1 receptors in the iris
mcqs.explanation: Atropine blocks M3 receptors in the constrictor pupillae → passive relaxation → pupil dilation (Passive Mydriasis). Active dilation occurs through contraction of the dilator pupillae via α1 stimulation.

mcqs.q: A 70-year-old patient with Parkinson's disease suffers from excessive drooling and tremors. Which drug is preferred?
mcqs.explanation: Benztropine crosses the blood-brain barrier and treats the central symptoms of Parkinson's disease such as tremors and sialorrhea. Glycopyrrolate is a quaternary ammonium compound and does not cross the barrier.

mcqs.q: Which of the following correctly describes the cardiac changes after a full therapeutic dose of Atropine?
options: Transient bradycardia followed by predominant tachycardia
mcqs.explanation: Low doses block presynaptic M1 → increased ACh → transient inhibition. Then M2 cardiac blockade dominates → tachycardia is the final effect.

mcqs.q: Which of the following Atropine effects lasts the longest (up to 7 days)?
mcqs.explanation: Atropine has a prolonged effect on the eye that can last up to 7 days (Mydriasis + Cycloplegia), which is much longer than its effects on other organs.

mcqs.q: Why is Atropine used together with Diphenoxylate in the treatment of diarrhea?
options: To enhance the antidiarrheal effect and reduce the potential for abuse
To absorb fluid from the intestines
mcqs.explanation: Diphenoxylate is an opioid that may be abused. Adding Atropine enhances the antidiarrheal effect and makes high doses intolerable (due to side effects), thereby reducing the potential for abuse.
It raises intraocular pressure, worsening glaucoma.

mcqs.q: The inhaled antimuscarinic bronchodilator for asthma/COPD is:
mcqs.explanation: Ipratropium is an inhaled antimuscarinic bronchodilator.
